data.title: DGRO: 11-Year Dividend Growth Streak Backed By Strong Fundamentals
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last reviewed the iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) on October 10, 2025, when I rated it a solid "hold" due to "its balanced mix of value, earnings growth, dividend growth, quality, and cost efficiency." I also found it likely to continue its impressive dividend growth streak, which is now up to 11, per Seeking Alpha.

If you're a manager or Index provider, it's fairly easy to manufacture dividend growth. All you need to do is routinely sell low-yielding stocks and replace them with higher-yielding ones, but if those replacements aren't on good financial footing, you might end up in a situation where you achieve your dividend growth objectives mainly by sacrificing total returns. Fortunately, DGRO is not one of those ETFs. Its components are delivering enough earnings growth to support future dividend growth, their payout ratios average just 42%, and in aggregate, they have increased dividends for over 22 years. DGRO has other strong fundamental characteristics that make it attractive to risk-averse investors, so for those reasons, I'm confident reiterating my solid "hold" rating on the fund. I hope you enjoy the read.

DGRO Overview

DGRO has $39 billion in assets under management, a 0.08% expense ratio, a 2.03% trailing dividend yield, and tracks the Morningstar US Dividend Growth Index, which aims to "provide exposure to securities in the Morningstar US Markets Index with a history of uninterrupted dividend growth and the capacity to sustain that growth." This description is spot on and confirms that the Index seeks companies with both a willingness and ability to increase their dividends. For the "ability" portion, the way they do that is by screening out potential yield traps (i.e., those with dividend yields in the top 10%) and insisting on positive consensus earnings forecasts and payout ratios below 75%. For the "willingness" portion, companies must be on a 5+ year dividend growth streak. That requirement might seem a little short, but the benefit is that it allows more high-growth tech stocks into the mix. Since earnings growth funds dividend growth, it helps the Index achieve its primary dividend growth objective.

The Index reconstitutes only once per year after the third Friday in December, so there's very little opportunity to manufacture dividend growth in the way I described earlier. However, DGRO's turnover rates (25-65% over the last five fiscal years) are higher compared to others like the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), which requires 10+ years of consecutive dividend increases. This makes sense though - since the five-year minimum is a lower bar to clear, more stocks can qualify.

Lastly, DGRO's components are dividend-dollar-weighted, which is essentially a blend between a cap-weighting scheme and a yield-weighting scheme. Weights are determined by the product of a stock's shares outstanding and indicated dividend yield, subject to a 3% cap, and the result of this is that several low-yielding stocks can receive high weights simply because they're so massive. That's how Apple Inc. (AAPL), a stock worth $3.8 trillion but yields just 0.40%, makes its way into DGRO's top ten list.

DGRO Performance Analysis

Since July 2014, one month following its inception, DGRO has delivered a 280.97% total return (including dividends), which works out to an 11.97% annualized return through April 15, 2026. That's slightly better than VIG's 11.48% result, and although it's behind SPY's 13.26% annualized gain, the chart below shows that SPY only really began to break away over the last couple of years. Low and non-dividend-payers like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) fueled these excess returns for SPY, and since it's a cap-weighted ETF, high concentration actually worked in its favor.

One downside for DGRO is that it didn't really offer investors much downside protection during the Q1 2020 COVID-19 crash. Over those three months, it declined by 21.92%, which wasn't much better than SPY's, and even though its "worst 12 months" figure was about 9% more, its risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios) were still lower. Therefore, I do think there is a slight total returns sacrifice investors must accept by prioritizing dividend growth, especially if you're bullish on the U.S. stock market over the long run.

Still, that sacrifice might be worth it. Per Portfolio Visualizer, a $10,000 investment in January 2017 with reinvested dividends would result in total portfolio income of $612 for the calendar year 2025. Interestingly, if one chose not to reinvest dividends, income would only be $502, so I think reinvestment is an important aspect. Therefore, it's probably best for younger investors looking to develop a steady stream of income later in life, while older investors with more immediate income needs might consider higher-yielding funds like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). Per Seeking Alpha, it's on a 14-year dividend growth streak, which is why it's often considered a benchmark ETF to own for DGI investors.

DGRO Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for DGRO's top 20 holdings, which comprise 42.25% of the portfolio. This "under-the-hood" analysis helps us understand why DGRO is well-positioned to continue its 11-year dividend growth streak, and at the same time, allows us to compare it with VIG, SCHD, and SPY on an apples-to-apples basis. Please have a quick scan of the results, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

The first thing to highlight is DGRO's 2.12% Index Yield, which is the weighted average dividend yield of its current components. After deducting its 0.08% expense ratio, shareholders should yield 2.04%, which actually lines up quite well with the fund's 2.03% trailing yield. Estimated yields for the other three ETFs are as follows:

  • VIG: 1.72% - 0.04% = 1.68%
  • SCHD: 3.54% - 0.06% = 3.48%
  • SPY: 1.19% - 0.08% = 1.11%

In short, VIG offers a better yield than VIG and SPY but is still miles away from SCHD, and this gap in starting yield can make a big difference if your time horizon is relatively short (e.g., if you're nearing retirement). Executing a successful dividend growth strategy requires time, and in the short term, it doesn't matter how strong DGRO's dividend growth rates are - you'll likely never get the same yield on cost as SCHD. As a simple example, the rule of 70 suggests you'll need a growth rate of about 7% to double your DGRO's yield on cost to 4.08% in 10 years. To get to the same 4.08% yield, SCHD only needs 1.6% annualized dividend growth over those 10 years, and based on its historical 3-, 5-, and 10-year growth rates, that shouldn't be a problem.

However, to reiterate an earlier point, how each ETF achieves its dividend growth matters, and in my opinion, DGRO's method is much healthier. To illustrate, its components' three-year historical and one-year estimated EPS growth rates of 6.33% and 11.21% support the high-single-digit dividend growth investors need without having to "manufacture" dividend growth by routinely rotating out of low-yielding stocks and replacing them with higher-yielding ones. DGRO's 41.87% portfolio-level payout ratio is also healthy, while VIG and SPY are in an even better position to support increased payments.

In contrast, SCHD's fundamentals are more questionable. For a moment, I ask that you imagine yourself building your own dividend growth portfolio using individual stocks. When screening, would you be comfortable picking a stock whose earnings per share have declined over the last three years? If not, then SCHD's -2.53% 3Y EPS CAGR should be a red flag. Yes, its components have still increased dividends by an annualized 7.02% over that period, but that dividend growth obviously isn't supported by earnings, and that's why its 58.85% payout ratio is so high. Not coincidentally, this earnings growth problem only started showing up in 2023, right around the same time SCHD began to underperform most of its peers.

GARP and Quality Considerations

Another angle I like to consider is which ETF offers the best growth potential for its price. To do so, I've calculated the following forward PEG ratios (price-earnings-growth) for each ETF using their portfolio-level P/E ratios and 1Y estimated EPS growth rates:

  • DGRO: 18.35x / 11.21% = 1.64x
  • VIG: 20.73x / 14.17% = 1.46x
  • SCHD: 15.18x / 6.54% = 2.32x
  • SPY: 21.42x / 18.46% = 1.16x

As shown below, DGRO's 1.64x result is third-best behind SPY and VIG but still much better than SCHD's 2.32x figure. Since I'm a big believer that a dividend growth ETF should have good earnings growth potential for its price, I give the edge to VIG at the moment, but that doesn't mean DGRO is a bad choice. The ratios are close, DGRO offers a higher starting yield, and overall, it's still in pretty good shape.

That said, I do believe DGRO's quality features are the weakest of the four. My ETF Rankings System has assigned it a quality score of only 6.41/10 compared to 7.46/10, 6.88/10, and 8.57/10 for VIG, SCHD, and SPY, respectively. Key contributors include relatively low free cash flow margins of 10.44% and lower returns on equity (25.98%) and total capital (14.21%), as shown below.

Lastly, I'd like to caution against owning both DGRO and VIG. As of today, they overlap by 70%, and I can see overlap getting pushed to 75-80% over the next five years. If this materializes, holding both would be a relatively inefficient use of capital, and to give you an idea of what stocks in DGRO might soon qualify in VIG once they hit the 10+ year dividend growth mark, I've compiled the following list of possible additions for the 2027 and 2028 calendar years.

Ticker Company Name Market Cap (In Millions) Indicated Dividend Yield Dividend Growth Years
AMAT Applied Materials, Inc. 315,706 0.53% 8
TMO Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. 185,982 0.38% 8

EOG EOG Resources, Inc. 73,168 2.99% 8

Many of these won't have a material impact on VIG's cap-weighting scheme, but stocks like Applied Materials and Thermo Fisher Scientific are big enough to earn weights of about 1% each, so I don't think 75-80% overlap in five years or so is much of a stretch.

Investment Recommendation

DGRO has a lot going for it. Its long-term total returns with reinvested dividends are excellent, and it has delivered on its dividend growth objective for the last 11 years. Furthermore, and most importantly, my fundamental analysis suggests this impressive dividend growth streak will continue, given how its portfolio-level earnings growth and dividend payout ratios are clearly in the safe zone. My only critiques are that its earnings growth rates, free cash flow margins, capital efficiency ratios, and forward PEG ratios are slightly inferior to VIG at the moment, and I'm not sure if that's worth sacrificing over an extra 0.36% in yield. However, I consider these critiques to be small, so if you're a long-time investor in DGRO, I don't see any reason to change gears now. Therefore, I'm reiterating my "hold" rating. Thank you for reading, and as always, I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: GAB Rights Offering Update: Lower Subscription Price And Deadline Extended
data.description:
Gabelli Equity Trust (GAB) has updated the terms of its ongoing rights offering. From the press release:

GREENWICH, Conn., March 31, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- The Gabelli Equity Trust (NYSE: GAB) (the “Fund”) today announced that is has updated the offering price of its previously announced Rights Offering (the “Offer”) and has extended the expiration date of the Offer.

Under the updated terms of the Offer, the Subscription Price will be $5.00 per share, replacing the prior Subscription Price of $5.50. This revised price reflects current market conditions and investor feedback.

In addition, the expiration date of the Offer has been extended to April 21, 2026, at 5:00 PM Eastern Time.

As previously announced, ten Rights enable a shareholder to purchase one additional share of common stock. Record date shareholders who fully exercise their Primary Subscription Rights will be eligible for an over-subscription privilege entitling these shareholders to subscribe, subject to certain limitations and a pro-rata allotment, for any additional common shares not purchased pursuant to the Primary Subscription at the same Subscription Price. The Rights will continue to trade on the New York Stock Exchange through April 20, 2026.

Important Notice to Shareholders Who Have Already Exercised Their Rights Through Their Broker: In light of the change in the Subscription Price and the expiration date, subscriptions previously submitted through brokers will be cancelled and shareholders who wish to remain in the Offer must contact the submitting broker to resubscribe at the current $5.00 Subscription Price prior to the expiration date.

We previously discussed the rights offering in a previous article.

In the updated offering, the subscription price is reduced to $5.00 (from $5.50) and the expiration date was extended to April 21, 2026. Ten rights still allow holders to subscribe for one new share, and the over-subscription privilege remains in place for holders who fully exercise their primary rights.

This adjustment matters because GAB's structure is a fixed-dollar subscription price, rather than the more typical rights offering formulas that links the subscription price to market price or NAV at expiry. Therefore, the decision analysis for subscribing is relatively straightforward: the higher the market price is relative to the subscription price, the more valuable the rights become.

At the current share price of $5.58, the updated $5.00 subscription price puts the offering significantly back "in-the-money". Under the previous $5.50 floor, benefit to subscription was minimal, since investors could buy in the market at nearly the same level. Consistent with this, the value of GAB's transferable rights had also dropped precipitously, from around 3 cents at open to around 0.4 cents just before the extension was announced. If GAB had fallen below $5.50, subscribing would have been uneconomical, and the rights would likely have become effectively worthless.

With the price reset to $5.00, subscribing again becomes economically attractive for shareholders, and the rights immediately regained their value - jumping up approximately tenfold from their lows. It was bad luck for anyone who sold rights near the bottom (and good luck for those who bought!), but hindsight is always 20/20; it wasn't really possible to have predicted either the possibility or timing of such an extension, nor the degree of adjustment to the new $5.00 level.

Important note: subscriptions already submitted through brokers will be cancelled because the terms changed. Anyone who previously exercised via a broker and still wants to participate must resubscribe at the new $5.00 price before the new deadline.

The previous analysis on the dilutive or accretive nature of this rights offering still applies, only with the subscription price adjusted. If the NAV is higher than $5.00 (i.e., the fund is trading at a discount) at expiry, the offering will be dilutive as new shares will be issued below NAV. On the other hand, if the NAV is below $5.00 (i.e., the fund is trading at a premium), the offering will be accretive as new shares will be issued above NAV.

Action plan

In summary, this is a basic action plan for GAB investors:

  1. Subscribe if:
    • GAB's share price remains above $5.00 at expiry.
    • You want to increase your position and believe in GAB long-term.
  2. Do NOT subscribe if:
    • GAB's share price falls below $5.00 at expiry.
    • It would be cheaper to buy shares directly on the open market instead.
  3. If you don't want to subscribe:
    • Sell your rights on the open market, as the value of the rights may potentially decline as the offering period progresses.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Forensic Look At The Private Credit Market
data.description:
One of investors’ biggest concerns about the U.S. economy today is the private credit market. I have a unique insight here, having started my career as a Bank Examiner examining Savings & Loans in Florida in 1985, and later worked as a Commercial Credit Manager of a mid-sized bank.

In this article, I will discuss:

  • prior credit cycles
  • the size and makeup of the private credit market,
  • exposure to software companies,
  • credit quality
  • impact on the economy,
  • investor reaction so far
  • the largest business development corps (BDCs)
  • risk profile of private credit versus bank loans
  • current risks, and
  • investor recommendations

Prior Credit Cycles

My first job in 1985 was as a government Bank Examiner in Florida, examining Savings & Loans. Remember them? It was a crazy time and place for that job, as the majority of the S&Ls there were in collapse mode due to bad commercial real estate loans. Between 1985 and 1992, over 2000 S&Ls and banks failed. It was worse for the S&Ls as they had just been granted authority to make commercial loans, and many didn’t really know what they were doing. Almost all of these failures were due to a nationwide commercial real estate bust.

Between 2008 and 2012, there was another credit cycle bust, and 465 banks failed. The headlines back then were all about bad subprime loans (higher risk residential mortgages). In reality, few banks failed due to subprime loans. The failures again were almost entirely from another commercial real estate bust. The subprime hit was huge, but mostly among the largest banks, insurance companies such as AIG, and foreign investors.

The point here is that almost all the bank failures in the past 75 years occurred primarily due to two commercial real estate busts, not non-real estate commercial loans, which most of private credit is. The most recent failures of 4 banks, including Silicon Valley and Signature Bank, a few years back, had little to do with lending at all.

Commercial lending has always been cyclical. Things occasionally break down, usually due to recessions. Underwriting afterward becomes tight for a while. As time goes on, due to competition, underwriting loosens up. The longer things go without a new recession, the looser credit standards get.

The 2020 Covid recession was too brief to cause much damage. In fact, all the government stimuli actually led to historically low defaults and delinquencies. We have now gone close to 20 years, and memories of a real economic downturn and credit bust are fading.

The takeaway here is that there are crosswinds. On the one hand, the cycle is getting long in the tooth, suggesting that underwriting standards are likely loosening. On the other hand, non-real estate loan defaults have not caused significant stress on the economy in over 75 years.

Private Credit Market

The first chart below, from Barclays PLC (BCS), shows that the Private Credit Market is about $1.7 trillion globally when including dry powder (undrawn lines and loans). It has also grown very rapidly in recent years.

This second chart by BIS shows it at $2.1 trillion. BIS is a research company owned by 65 Central Banks.

The next chart, also by Barclays, shows that the majority of the market is in the U.S. at about $1.1 trillion. That is the same size as the subprime market was in 2007, according to the International Monetary Fund

While that sounds scary, keep in mind that U.S. GDP back then was only 46% what it is now. So, the real overall size is just under half what subprime was in 2007.

BDCs, have grown private credit loans at a hyper-growth rate over the past 5 years. They represent about 25-30% of private credit holders. That means their private credit loans have not yet been tested by maturities or a credit cycle.

Software Exposure

I have seen several estimates of software exposure in private credit, and they tend to range from 15% to 30% of the total. This first graph from Morgan Stanley shows it to be about 26% for Business Development Companies and 19% for CLOs. This is higher than in other leveraged loan types.

The second chart shows exposure to software firms by four large BDCs. The Wall Street Journal recently investigated and reported that software exposures are higher than the companies are reporting.

Credit Quality

Credit quality (the amount of delinquencies, defaults, restructures, or deterioration) has worsened due to increased leverage, but is not alarming at this point.

The first chart shows how it worsened. The BDC debt-to-equity ratio has tripled over the past 15 years.

The second chart shows no deterioration in default rates. In fact, if anything, defaults are declining. The dotted lines show selective defaults. Selective defaults are distressed exchanges, maturity extensions, and the substitution of payment-in-kind for cash interest. They are currently in decline. However, these recent declines may be due to the large recent charge-offs discussed below.

Investor Reaction

Despite no deterioration in credit quality, investors are running for the exits. The first chart shows a huge jump in BDC redemptions in the last 2 quarters. In the most recent quarter, the BDCs started placing withdrawal limits on investors.

The second chart shows which BDCs are not honoring some withdrawal requests. Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) has been the poster child for this phenomenon, but most others have joined them. The reason they have done this is that loans are much less liquid than stocks. Many redemptions at once cause liquidity problems for BDCs.

When investors lose the liquidity they expect, it usually has an impact on stock prices. The chart below shows a sudden drop in BDC values below book value.

The last chart in this section is broader; it covers all loans. Investors are getting skittish about credit defaults in general.

So why are investors bailing on private credit so suddenly when delinquencies are low? There are two primary reasons: fear that AI will disrupt the software industry private credit has so much exposure to, and several recent high-profile large losses. In the past 18 months, lenders have taken large losses on $11.5 billion in loans to First Brands, $2.0 billion in loans to Tricolor, $1.2 billion in loans to Pluralsight, and $1.5 billion in loans to Alacrity.

These large losses led JPM CEO Jamie Dimon last Fall to say some firms are “doing dumb things” and raised concerns about “cockroaches” in private credit.

Business Development Corps (BDCs)

BDCs hold about 25-30% of all private credit loans and are the most visible private credit holders. They are also the only pure-play private credit lender. For everyone else, including insurance companies, pensions, banks, and sovereign wealth, private credit is a small fraction of their holdings. As such, they are much more exposed than any other holder. The following chart shows a snapshot of the largest publicly traded Private Credit BDCs.

As shown above, the average discount to net asset value is currently 22.2%, and the average change in price over the last 6 months is 10.5%. Most of these stocks have actually spiked in the past week or so. A 22% discount indicates the market is worried but not too worried. Leverage is much lower than that of the banks. While the average bank is leveraged 10 to 1, the BDCs are only leveraged just over 2 to 1. This is because their loan portfolios are riskier. FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK) is the most beaten-down stock. Its non-accruals spiked last quarter, and it slashed its dividend. OTF is the next most beaten down, probably due to a higher concentration of software borrowers.

Where the Exposure Is

While the largest exposure is with the BDCs, pensions, endowments, sovereign wealth funds, insurance companies, and foundations also have significant exposure to private credit. Many of the BDCs are not publicly traded, as they are available only to institutional investors and high-net-worth individuals; except for insurance companies, these entities tend to be unlevereged, and any impact there may take years to be felt. Also, what happens to endowments, sovereign wealth funds, and foundations has little impact on individual investors, the stock market or the economy. After BDCs, insurance companies are the most exposed. In 2004, the IMF estimated that 3.5% of insurance companies' investments were in private credit. That is not enough to endanger most insurance firms, but it is potentially quite problematic.

There is also bank lending to BDCs. Three of the largest U.S. banks — JP Morgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Citigroup Inc. (C), and Wells Fargo & Company (WFC) — recently disclosed an exposure of just over $100 billion in lending to Private Credit lenders such as BDCs. They had a combined net worth of $759 billion on December 31, 2025, indicating large losses would be felt, but do not threaten the bank’s solvency.

Private Credit Versus Bank Lending

There are several differences between private credit and bank lending.

BDCs, in particular, have greater transparency than banks, as they usually list who the loans are made to, whereas banks rarely do. But most of their borrowers are not publicly traded, so there is little additional financial information available.

Several factors make private credit significantly riskier than bank lending.

  1. Private credit has, for the most part, not experienced a real recession. It was a much smaller market in the last real recession back in 2007-2009.
  2. Private credit lenders are not examined by government Bank Examiners. I was a Bank Examiner at the start of my career, and can vouch that they closely review most of the bank’s larger credits and a sample of the smaller ones. They require banks to write down loans much more quickly than the BDCs or any other non-FDIC insured lender.
  3. The interest rate on private credit loans is usually significantly higher than on bank loans. The higher the interest rate, the higher the risk. Borrowers usually go to private credit only if they can’t get bank financing, because it’s much more expensive. Investors buy private credit loans because yields of 8-14% are currently double those of investment-grade bonds and even higher than most junk bonds.
  4. Private credit has grown 4-5x faster than bank loans in recent years. In my experience, a lender growing that fast is usually taking a lot of risk and has a much higher likelihood of blowing up in a recession. The bank failures I mentioned earlier were heavily skewed toward the fastest-growing banks.

Risks

The biggest immediate risk appears to be with software borrowers. As shown above, software firms are a large portion of private creditors’ portfolios. The risk here is that AI disrupts many of those firms. This has already caused the stock prices of many publicly traded software firms to crash over the past 6 months. However, it is premature to know for sure. It may turn out that AI helps more than hurts most software firms.

Another big risk is a recession. Recessions usually lead to elevated loan delinquencies and charge-offs. It depends on how deep the recession is. For example, the Covid recession of 2020 only lasted 2 months and had little impact on delinquencies. On the other hand, the prior recession in 2007-2009 led to over 400 bank failures. As I detailed in the prior section, private credit loans are significantly riskier than bank loans. During a major recession, all lenders experience elevated delinquencies. The higher the risk, the more the defaults. The recent rapid growth in private credit only heightens this risk. In the past, those with the fastest loan growth tended to experience the largest loan losses during recessions.

Liquidity is already a problem for the BDCs. If investors fear reduced or negative returns, they demand their money back. Recently, most BDCs have had limited redemptions. If redemptions continue at this rate, the BDCs may be forced to dump loans at significant discounts. That could lead to large losses.

Private credit customers tend to be mid-market in size. That means they are smaller than most of the publicly traded companies. A recession can hit them harder as they tend to be less diversified. If they are using private credit, we also already know they are more leveraged.

Impact on the Economy

In a worst-case scenario, the impact would be large but not anywhere as large as the double hit in 2007-2009 from subprime and commercial real estate. Over 400 banks went under back then, mostly from commercial real estate. Only the largest banks have significant exposure to lending to the BDCs.

The primary lending concern right now is private credit. We also have a commercial real estate concern, primarily with office buildings, but also due to overbuilding in multi-family. But it’s nowhere as big as back then. The current private credit market is half the size, in real terms, as it was for subprime back then.

Without a recession, a private equity blowup would be mostly impacted by what happens to software. Loans to software companies through private credit are about $200-300 billion in the U.S. Most of the losses there would be taken by the BDCs and insurance companies, and to a lesser degree, foundations, pensions, and sovereign wealth funds. Shareholders outside BDCs and insurance companies would feel little impact, though there may be some at the largest banks. The exposure here is not large enough to put the U.S. into a recession by itself.

If there is a software blowup and a recession, the impact would be larger and would exacerbate the recession. However, any losses there could be offset by government spending.

Take Away

The amount of deterioration in the private credit market primarily comes down to two things. The first is how quickly and to what extent AI disrupts the software industry. It’s certainly possible that there is little disruption and software companies gain more from AI than they lose. The second is whether the U.S. enters a significant recession. I don’t believe a worst-case software scenario is large enough to put the U.S. into a recession by itself.

A large portion of private credit is held by the BDCs. The Van Eck BDC Income Fund ETF (BIZD) is down 6% over the past 6 months. It has $1.5 billion in assets and pays a 13.0% dividend. I provided a list of the largest publicly traded BDCs, most of which are held by BIZD. They are down 10.5% over the past 6 months and trading 22.2% below NAV. Given the elevated risks around software company exposure. It appears about right to slightly low to me based on what we know now. I rate the BDC industry as a hold right now.

If you own BDCs, continue to monitor developments in publicly traded software companies. If growth slows significantly, that is a red flag. The smaller software companies lent to by the BDCs are probably more at risk than the larger publicly traded ones. Another item to monitor is the pace of redemptions. If they continue at this pace, the BDCs will be forced to dump loans at a loss.

data.title: Unstoppables: 3 Top Stocks That Crushed The Market Over The Last 5 Years
data.description:
Long-Term Fundamentals: Enduring the Best and Worst of Times

The S&P 500 and Nasdaq climbed to record highs this week as investors grew optimistic about progress in the U.S.-Iran peace talks, supported by positive economic data and a strong start to earnings season. This comes on the heels of a market gripped by extreme fear and headline-driven volatility, as the conflict in the Middle East escalated, oil prices surged, gold plunged, and interest rate expectations shifted.

The recent uptick aligns with favorable post-crisis trends I discussed in my piece on the Best 5 Tech Stocks With Average Forward EPS Growth Of 197%. Panic selling during downturns can lead to financial losses and missed opportunities. The market has rebounded from geopolitical shocks dating back to Pearl Harbor. In fact, the S&P 500 has typically posted positive returns within 3 to 12 months after such events.

Trying to time the market for short-term gains can be difficult and risky, especially when betting on stocks with weak fundamentals. Now might be a good time to take a longer-term view and identify stocks that have endured the best of times and the worst of times. In addition to solid competitive advantages, stocks that outperform during favorable and challenging market conditions possess exceptional fundamentals, such as robust earnings growth, solid margins, and strong return on capital – along with bullish analyst revisions. Seeking Alpha’s quantitative tools can help investors find these types of stocks.

How I Chose The Top ‘Unstoppable’ Stocks In The Past 5 Years

Using Seeking Alpha’s Stock Screener, I selected Strong Buy stocks with the highest five-year price returns. I only kept stocks that beat the S&P 500 in the past five calendar years and year-to-date. The three stocks in my basket returned more than 4,400%, 2,000%, and 640%, respectively, in the past five years.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings are generated by a proprietary model that analyzes more than 100 metrics for each stock relative to sector peers and assigns grades across five factors: Growth, Value, Profitability, Revisions, and Momentum.

In the section below, I highlight context, recent developments, and key metrics supporting each stock’s Quant Rating.

1. Celestica Inc. (CLS)

  • Market Capitalization: $43.94B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Electronic Manufacturing Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/2026): 11 out of 530

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/2026): 2 out of 18

One of Alpha Picks' top-performing stocks, Celestica has delivered nearly 4,400% in total returns in the past five years, crushing the market and sector. A developer of advanced technology solutions for hyperscalers, Celestica is well positioned to gain from growing AI infrastructure investments. Shares climbed last month after CLS and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) said they were collaborating to bring the Helios rack-scale AI platform to market. Celestica will design and build networking switches optimized for large-scale AI clusters for AMD’s next-generation GPUs.

CLS vs. S&P 500 Tech Sector (XLK): 5Y Price Return

Beating earnings for 16 straight quarters, Celestica delivered strong results for fiscal year 2025, with revenue growing by 28% YoY to $12.39B, driven by AI-related demand in its connectivity and cloud solutions segment. CLS raised its FY 2026 revenue guidance to $17B, as it continues to align with customer plans for long-term AI infrastructure investments.

BNP projects CLS revenue and earnings to surge through CY 2028, citing growing capacity and engineering capabilities that could lead to higher-margin business in the networking segment. Celestica is poised to gain from data center capex spending and demand for switches to support AI workloads, BNP analyst Karl Ackerman said in a note to clients.

Backed by 15 upward earnings revisions to no downward revisions, Celestica’s earnings and revenue are expected to surge in the next three fiscal years. Celestica’s forward EPS long-term growth rate (3-5Y CAGR) of ~43% vs. the sector’s 16% underpins an A- Growth Grade, while standout return on equity and capital support solid profitability scores.

Celestica is trading at a premium to earnings, driven by explosive momentum in the past year, but at a discount to the sector of 28% based on its forward PEG - a ratio that combines price/earnings and expected growth. CLS is poised to continue riding AI structural tailwinds as exceptional earnings upside and momentum drive its Strong Buy rating.

2. Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (STRL)

  • Market Capitalization: $13.99B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction and Engineering

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/2026): 22 out of 610

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/2026): 2 out of 36

Another member of the Alpha Picks portfolio, Sterling has undergone a strategic transformation with a larger focus on e-infrastructure solutions, driven by strong demand for data center projects. Alongside soaring momentum, Sterling’s earnings grew by a CAGR of 44% in the past five years as it posted significant increases in operating profit and levered free cash flow.

STRL vs. S&P 500 Industrial Sector (XLI): 5Y Price Return

High data center demand and geographic expansion drove solid growth in revenue and operating income in FY 2025, with particular strength across electrical and site development work in the Texas market. E-infrastructure solutions accounted for 59% of total revenue - up from a 44% share in the prior year.

Sterling ended the year with a $3B backlog, marking a 78% increase vs. last year, with most of the orders consisting of data center, large manufacturing, and semiconductor projects. With robust order visibility and market tailwinds, Sterling expects to continue strong growth into 2026, a positive outlook supported by consensus estimates.

EPS is projected to rise by nearly 25% in FY 2026, and revenue by nearly 22% to $3.04B, as surging forward EBIT and earnings support solid metric grades. Sterling’s B+ Profitability Grade is underpinned by a return on capital of nearly 20% and ROE of 30% – both well above sector medians and signifying efficient deployment of resources.

The stock’s outstanding price performance has pressured its valuation grade in the past three months, but forward PEG is in line with the sector median. Sterling has demonstrated a strong ability to endure market fluctuations and deliver consistent returns to shareholders.

3. Fabrinet (FN)

  • Market Capitalization: $24.57B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Electronic Manufacturing Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/16/2026): 13 out of 530

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/16/2026): 3 out of 18

Joining the Alpha Picks portfolio in March, Fabrinet provides advanced electronic and optical technologies with a focus on the high-performance computing HPC, telecom, industrial laser, medical, and automotive markets, benefitting from shortages in AI data center components and growing demand from hyperscalers.

FN 5Y Price Return

Strength across the data center interconnect and telecom segments drove record Q2 2026 earnings, as revenue surged by 36% YoY to an all-time high of $1.13B. Fabrinet expects continued growth in telecom, datacom, and HPC in the third quarter, with strong operating leverage offsetting FX headwinds, as it targets new opportunities to produce transceivers directly for hyperscalers.

Backed by eight upward earnings revisions in the last three months to no downward revisions, for a top-notch Revisions Grade, Fabrinet’s EPS is expected to grow by roughly 33% in FY 2026 and revenue by 32% to $4.54B. Major growth in capex investments to expand capacity resulted in lower free cash flow, which has weighed on Fabrinet’s growth and profitability scores. However, Fabrinet has demonstrated strong historical and forward growth in revenue and operating profit, in addition to robust return on capital and equity.

Although the stock is trading at a premium to the sector, collective strength across all factor grades - especially momentum and earnings revisions - continues to support a Strong Buy rating, which Fabrinet has held for five straight months. Fabrinet is well positioned for more growth in earnings, revenue, and operating profit, driven by high demand for AI infrastructure components. Another stock with strong fundamentals and growth prospects, Fabrinet wraps up my basket of Strong Buy stocks that have crushed the market in the past five years.

3 Top Stocks With Market-Crushing Long-Term Returns

Investors naturally tend to focus on short-term price movements during geopolitical shocks, but often lose sight of the key fundamentals that drive long-term price appreciation. And panic selling has led to financial losses as the market has a history of recovering within three to 12 months after geopolitical events dating back to Pearl Harbor. Shifting from a short-term focus, I decided to look at the best quant-rated stocks that have a long history of consistent returns and beating the market. SA Quant identified three Strong Buy stocks that crushed the market in the past five years, delivering returns ranging from about 640% to more than 4,400%.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives.
