Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top 'strong buy' quant stocks. Check out our new Alpha Picks app in the Apple App Store! Seeking Alpha's quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you're looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It's a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy investing!

data.title: ASML Q1 2026 Earnings Update
data.description:
A new earnings season has arrived. If ASML’s earnings is any guide, be prepared to hear another quarter of “demand is outstripping supply” from all the players in the semi value chain.

ASML’s (ASML) CEO, Christophe Fouquet, started his prepared remarks with the following, which set the tone:

“…for the foreseeable future, demand will continue to outpace supply. This creates constraints across end markets from AI to mobile and PCs, which is driving our customers to aggressively add capacity.”

The last question on the call was about whether ASML may become the bottleneck for supply to get closer to demand. Fouquet does not think that is the case:

I know the question of bottleneck comes back very often. I think we don’t feel at all that we are the bottleneck today. We’re very closely working with our customer. And again, we have many, many, many tools in our hands to make sure we keep it this way.

During the call, ASML management explained how much they are expanding capacity to meet the growing demand (emphasis mine):

last year, we had 44 tools. If you are just looking at 80 tools, we say at least 80, but if you just look at 80 tools, those 80 tools give you double the wafer per hour capacity that we would have shipped in 2025. And on top of that, we’re helping customers upgrade their installed base… we’re really working hand in glove with the customers to look at what is your capacity need, what’s the easiest way and also the most economical way for you to get to the productivity that you need. And we’re executing on all fronts on availability, on productivity, on unit numbers, capacity and then upgrading the installed base.

I find it quite remarkable how quickly ASML was able to expand the capacity despite having a maze-like supply chain. For context, ASML had 5,150 suppliers in 2024; therefore, ramping up capacity means ASML needs most of these suppliers to also step up their game simultaneously to meet ASML’s wish list.

It is, of course, not magic; rather, ASML’s own meticulous preparedness across their supply chain that helped their ability to expand the capacity in a relatively short time. From the call (emphasis mine):

“I think we have been mostly explaining in the last few years that we were preparing the supply chain basically to be able to go to a capability of 90 on Low NA and to a capability of 600 for deep UV, total deep UV. And I think what we see in these ramps is that a lot of the preparation is paying off. So I think that, of course, you always have challenge with the supply chain. But I will say, so far, our supply chain has been able to support our move rate increase quarter-by-quarter. And that includes, by the way, ZEISS to name them, that include the optic, where I think we had major challenges a few years ago in the previous ramps. I think we are in a much better shape there.”

It also helps that given Samsung (SSNLF) and Intel’s (INTC) ability to attract customers in light of the compute-constrained environment as well as potential geopolitical risks for relying solely on TSMC (TSM) over the long run, TSMC’s monopsony power over ASML is almost certainly not increasing. From the call:

“It’s pretty clear that the demand in the foundry business is huge and is outweighing the supply, right? So that leaves a bit of room for others than the market leader. And I think that’s room that the other players are trying to enter into. We all know about the plans of Samsung in Taylor. So that’s real. And of course, that also requires shipment for us, which is happening. The U.S. player in this business already has quite some capacity, I would say. So we’ve said it before that for this year, we’re not counting on a huge number of shipments in that regard because they already have quite a bit of capacity.Then you ask about the longer term. Of course, the market that is characterized by multiple players at least will sort of guarantee innovation. And that, I think, is what is important. I think the market leader has been tremendously innovative. So you cannot say that even in the market that was dominated by one player, you would still see a lot of innovation. I think that’s what we’ve seen in the past couple of years. But having 3 players in there will probably guarantee even more innovation. And I ultimately think that, that is good for the ecosystem.”

If there are multiple players and one of them adopts high NA EUV, which then yields better results than others who do not, you can see how a oligopoly instead of a monopoly in advanced logic chips can potentially increase the pace of high NA EUV adoption. If it remains just a TSMC monopoly, the pace of adoption of high NA EUV can be dictated by solely on TSMC’s wishes.

Of course, logic chips are only half of ASML’s system sales, while the other half comes from memory companies. While listening to the call yesterday, I almost thought ASML management was pitching memory stocks! ASML has seen a major adoption of EUV among memory players, and management made the case that the move from low NA to high NA EUV will also play out similarly over time as the customers see the performance and productivity benefits over time. From the call (emphasis mine):

I think DRAM has been a bit the perfect storm for ASML because, of course, we have this capacity buildup. But as we mentioned a few times, we have seen a major adoption of EUV in DRAM in 2025. And you may have noticed that our, I will say, U.S. DRAM customer also made this announcement that they were shifting also pretty strongly on EUV. And the reason for that is, of course, performance, but it’s also capacity because if you are going to use more EUV layers, you are going to need less multi-patterning and multi-patterning takes a lot of space also in the fab.…Now of course, what’s true for Low NA today, I think we expect to be true for High NA in the future. So it’s, again, not prime time for High NA today. But I can only say that more Low NA EUV adoption today can only help for more High NA adoption in the future because the logic of High NA is the same. It’s going to single expose, simplifying the process, getting more space, et cetera, et cetera. So I think DRAM has been really a good story when it comes to litho intensity in ‘25. And I think it’s translating very strongly into EUV demand this year and most probably in the years to come.

One question investors often wonder is whether ASML is leaving too much money on the table in a compute-constrained environment by not sufficiently increasing prices. ASML management declined to take advantage of its customers for what could turn out to be a temporary phenomenon. From the call (emphasis mine):

“Now in our model of pricing, as you know, our model of pricing is not based on the squeeze that our customers find ourselves in. That’s not the way we do business. The way we do business is that we look at the value that we provide to our customers, generation on generation, tool on tool, and we take our fair share in that. And you might say in the current climate, can’t you squeeze out a little bit more? I understand that. But it’s also true that when the market is good, it goes down a little bit and the customers are going through more difficult times that it also pays these fees. So fundamentally, we believe that the model that we have is a fair model. It’s also a model that is fair to all the players because I would find it difficult to explain why we’re charging more in, let’s say, the memory environment versus the logic environment. That’s just not the way we do business. So we’re very, very happy with the business model we have, which is based on the value of our tools, and we would gladly continue with that approach.”

Indeed, while ASML’s customers (TSMC and Micron (MU), for example) have higher gross margins today than ASML, looking at more long-term gross margin trajectory clearly depicts ASML’s more steady gross margin while memory companies’ margin whipsaw from one extreme to another. Even if TSMC’s gross margins were more volatile than ASML’s over the last decade, it is true that TSMC’s gross margin was almost consistently higher than ASML, perhaps a reflection of their monopsony power over ASML.

ASML increased the revenue guidance from EUR 34-39 billion to EUR 36-40 billion for 2026. Even late last year, analysts were expecting only EUR 33 billion in revenue for 2026, which has now increased ~20% over the last 6 months. Even though the stock has gone down a bit following the earnings, the stock is still +25% YTD. My broad takeaway from the ASML call was that if you are looking to get a hint of capex peaking or demand-supply gap narrowing during any of the earnings calls in the semi value chain, you are unlikely to get it during this earnings season.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: RPV: A Deep Value ETF, Sector Mix With Increased Cyclical Exposure
data.description:
The Invesco S&P Pure Value ETF (RPV) offers investors a portfolio with very low valuations, thanks to an investment approach that favors companies with strong value profiles. This is a characteristic of its investment design that is exacerbated at this moment, given the fund’s increased exposure to cyclical and value areas like energy, basic materials, and industrials compared to the picture in my previous review of this fund in early 2025. Of course, RPV’s valuation gives it some appeal at this present time, given current uncertainties on the macro front. The flip side is a long-term track record that is not among the most compelling alternatives from a total-return perspective. With that in mind, the following sections will break down the fund’s core holdings and compare its sector positioning, valuation, and performance with the S&P 500 and other value ETFs.

ETF Description and Highlights

RPV provides targeted exposure to U.S. companies with the strongest value characteristics within the S&P 500 universe. The fund follows a rules-based methodology, tracking the S&P 500 Pure Value Index. As the first step, companies are assigned a value score, which incorporates metrics such as book-to-price, earnings-to-price, and sales-to-price ratios, and also a growth score, based on measures like historical sales and earnings growth, and 1-year price performance. Stocks are then ranked based on the relative balance between these scores, with only those exhibiting the strongest value bias included in the final index, as the index filters out stocks that exhibit a blended style. Once selected, stocks are weighted according to the strength of their value characteristics, resulting in higher allocations to stocks trading at lower valuations on a relative basis. The index is reconstituted and rebalanced annually to reflect current companies’ financials and market conditions.

As of April 2, 2026, RPV had $1.7 billion in assets under management, invested in a portfolio of 121 companies, with an average market capitalization of $32.2 billion. The fund’s allocation is heavily tilted toward mid-cap stocks, which account for 71.2% of total assets, followed by large caps at 13.5% and small caps at 13.9%, while mega caps represent only 1.1%. This distribution is in line with the picture in January 2025, when mid caps already represented 68.6% of the fund, and remains in sharp contrast to the parent S&P 500, where mega and large caps represent over 80% of the index, given its market-cap-weighted structure.

RPV’s top ten holdings are Dow Inc. (DOW) (2.8%), Bunge Global SA (BG) (2.76%), Archer-Daniels-Midland Company (ADM) (2.22%), LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (LYB) (2.18%), Mosaic Company (MOS) (2.01%), Tyson Foods, Inc. (TSN) (1.94%), Ford Motor Company (F) (1.73%), The Cigna Group (CI) (1.69%), General Motors Company (GM) (1.67%), and Target Corporation (TGT) (1.59%), in a relatively concentrated combination of materials and consumer-oriented sectors.

The table below lists a group of value-oriented ETFs that will serve as the peer set for RPV in this analysis. The largest fund is the Vanguard Value Index Fund ETF (VTV), with more than $200 billion in assets, tracking a market-cap-weighted index. Next, the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) follows a similar capitalization-weighted approach, selecting companies from the Russell 1000 with value characteristics, while the iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE) tracks the S&P 500 Value Index, a subset of the S&P 500, similar to RPV. The Dimensional US Marketwide Value ETF (DFUV) has a different investment style, applying a multi-factor process that emphasizes value and profitability measures. Meanwhile, the WisdomTree U.S. Value ETF (WTV) employs an earnings-weighted methodology. Lastly, the Vanguard Mega Cap Value Index Fund ETF (MGV) focuses on the upper end of the market, tracking the CRSP US Mega Cap Value Index.

RPV’s largest exposures are concentrated in financial services (17.2%), consumer staples (15.8%), and healthcare (14.8%), forming the core of the portfolio and highlighting a clear tilt toward defensive and income-oriented segments. Although RPV’s position in healthcare remains significant, it has dropped significantly from the 19.1% in January 2025, when it was the fund’s largest sector. A second tier includes energy (11.5%), consumer discretionary (10.4%), and basic materials (9.6%), while smaller allocations are spread across industrials (6.2%) and communication services (6.2%), utilities (4.3%), technology (2.6%), and real estate (1.5%). We can see the fund now has greater exposure to cyclical and value areas like energy, basic materials, and industrials, which increased nearly 2% each from the picture in early 2025.

As a result of the current sector mix, the fund is now overweight in consumer staples (+10.6%), basic materials (+7.7%), and energy (+7.6%), reflecting an even stronger value bias, while it remains significantly underweight in technology (-31.4%), and in communication services (-4.3%) and industrials (-2.3%) to a lesser degree, illustrating a positioning away from typical growth segments.

If we break down RPV’s allocation across its largest sectors, we see that in financial services, the largest holdings are in asset management and custody banks through names like Everest Group, Ltd. (EG), Franklin Resources, Inc. (BEN), and Invesco Ltd. (IVZ). Insurance is another meaningful segment, led by Prudential Financial, Inc. (PRU), American International Group, Inc. (AIG), and MetLife, Inc. (MET). The fund also has a solid presence in regional banking with core holdings in institutions like Citizens Financial Group, Inc. (CFG) and M&T Bank Corporation (MTB). This is a kind of portfolio that has strong sensitivity to the yield curve and credit conditions, especially the banking segment, as holdings are smaller players rather than global names like Citibank or Bank of America, for instance. This leaves the portfolio more exposed to a softer economy or credit deterioration that would hit sentiment quickly, though holdings are expected to remain attractive by nature from a valuation perspective.

In consumer staples, the allocation is spread across several food-related segments, with overweight exposure to agricultural products through Bunge Global and Archer-Daniels-Midland Company (ADM). That is followed by packaged foods, where Tyson Foods, Inc. (TSN) and Conagra Brands, Inc. (CAG) are the core positions. Elsewhere, the fund owns food retailers such as supermarket operator Kroger Co. (KR), food distributor Sysco Corporation (SYY), along with packaged foods players Kraft Heinz Company (KHC) and Hormel Foods Corporation (HRL). In summary, RPV is leaning into food, distribution, and agricultural businesses that may hold up better in a slow-growth or even recessionary environment. That said, this part of the portfolio is still exposed to input-cost pressures, including freight costs if oil prices remain elevated for a prolonged time.

The exposure to healthcare has a slightly more balanced mix, though the managed healthcare segment anchors the sector, led by health insurer Cigna Group (CI) and government-focused managed-care player Centene Corporation (CNC). Core positions still include Medicare-focused insurer Humana Inc. (HUM) and distributors Cencora, Inc. (COR) and Cardinal Health, Inc. (CAH). Meanwhile, a few holdings in pharmaceuticals round out the portfolio, including Viatris Inc. (VTRS) and Pfizer Inc. (PFE). As a result, the fund ends up with a sector positioning that underweights the biotech segment, while favoring companies with more cash-flow visibility, like managed care and distributors, and pharmaceuticals that usually carry some defensive appeal, which can bode well for the fund in a softer growth environment like we are now.

Besides these largest sectors, other still significant allocations include energy players like refiner Valero Energy Corporation (VLO), exploration and production firm APA Corporation (APA), automakers Ford and General Motors Company (GM), and basic materials producers with holdings in Dow Inc. (DOW), DuPont de Nemours, Inc. (DD), and commodity chemicals supplier LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (LYB), and fertilizer producer Mosaic Company (MOS). Of course, holdings in financial services and materials carry some cyclical exposure, but consumer staples and parts of healthcare offer much more defensiveness to the overall portfolio. That mix may hold up reasonably well if the current environment stays choppy. However, it may also leave the fund dependent on a somewhat narrow macro backdrop: weak enough growth to help value, yet not so weak that earnings in cyclical sectors come under pressure.

RPV’s sector positioning has a distinct low exposure to technology, even compared to value-oriented funds, as peers maintain double-digit allocations to the sector, making RPV’s 2.6% allocation a clear outlier. This gap is especially pronounced relative to IVE, which keeps a significant exposure to technology despite its value orientation.

On the other hand, RPV shows a heavier allocation to consumer staples (+6.6%) and basic materials (+6.1%), especially compared to peers such as IWD and DFUV. At the same time, the fund also maintains solid exposure to financial services, broadly in line with the group, as all peers keep a significant position in this typical value segment.

As a result, the overall sector correlation is only moderate, ranging from 0.33 to 0.63 across peers. The dispersion is largely driven by RPV’s clearly limited position in technology, along with its heavier tilt toward defensive and cyclical value sectors, which gives the fund a somewhat more exacerbated defensive bias relative to the peer group.

Very Low Valuation

At 11.6x P/E, RPV trades at a steep discount of about 44% relative to the S&P 500’s 20.6x. Unlike the benchmark, which is heavily influenced by high-multiple technology stocks, RPV’s limited position in the sector is a key factor driving the fund’s valuation down. Another significant contribution comes from a stock selection that favors low multiple names, leading to the fund’s over 40% discount in core sectors such as financial services and consumer staples relative to the benchmark. The fund’s overweight positions in companies like General Motors and Ford in a premium sector like consumer discretionary illustrate this point very well.

If valuation is compelling, growth is a clear trade-off. The fund’s historical earnings growth of -4.5% falls well short of the S&P 500’s 10.1%, and its 6.1% sales growth also trails the benchmark’s 7.3%, with a lot of that driven by the sector mix, as the fund avoids high-growth areas like technology and communication services. We can see a similar story in profitability. RPV’s 17.1% EBITDA margin is roughly half the benchmark’s 35.4%, and its 8.5% ROE is nearly four times lower than the index’s 44.0%. That gap, though, seems consistent with the fund’s large discounts to the S&P 500. Again, low exposure to technology and communication services plays a key role, while individual holdings also drag EBITDA and ROE down almost equally across all sectors.

Broadly speaking, RPV’s appeal lies in its deep value mandate. The flip side is that its exposure to names like Ford, General Motors, CVS Health (CVS), Bunge, and Archer Daniels Midland, usually operating with thinner margins, ends up weighing on the fund’s overall profitability measures. Looking ahead, the same allocation dynamic is likely to persist in most sectors where the fund is overweight. The exceptions may occur in areas like energy, where higher margins coming from elevated oil prices could boost the companies’ profitability, leading to outperformance over the broader market as well. At the same time, higher oil prices may impact input costs in the materials sectors, pressuring margins to some degree. That said, changes in the portfolio will be tied to how stock prices will respond to these factors. If energy stocks do not increase in the same proportion as earnings, we may see these companies accounting for a greater share of the fund. Likewise, if stock prices outgrow earnings, a higher P/E will lead to lighter exposure to the sector.

With most ETFs in the peer group in the 17x to 19x range, we can arguably say that RPV trades in deep value territory. Other funds also trading at very low valuations, though still above RPV, include WTV (12.8x P/E) and DFUV (14.7x), as these funds benefit from a sector mix that also overweights energy and limits the exposure to an expensive sector like consumer discretionary. In addition, their large positioning in small caps helps keep valuations low, which is something also visible in RPV, where small and mid caps account for nearly 85% of the fund, the largest within the group. That being said, RPV, WV, and DFUV’s lower valuations make sense, as they reflect these funds’ below-average historical earnings growth rates.

Modest Performance

RPV’s annualized returns of 15.4% (3-year) and 10.1% (5-year) have both trailed the S&P 500 by a significant margin. The fund’s performance has also been slightly below the peer group across these periods, with mid-range returns, in line with peers like IWD and IVE, but lagging WTV and MGV. The most challenging periods for RPV are quite clear, particularly in 2020, when it lagged the benchmark by a wide 27 percentage points, and again in 2023–2024, when it struggled to keep pace with the strong rally in growth-oriented segments. The main bright spot came in 2022, when the fund held up significantly better than both the benchmark and peers during the market drawdown, sparked by higher interest rates. Of note, RPV has done better than the S&P 500 year-to-date, given the generally risk-off mood driven first by concerns about potential disruptions in many industries due to the AI revolution and more recently by the conflict in the Middle East.

On the other hand, with a beta of 0.95 (3-year) and 0.87 (5-year), RPV has been somewhat less volatile than the broader market, though still slightly above most peers, especially VTV and MGV, likely influenced by RPV’s substantial exposure to more volatile mid- and small-cap stocks. Its upside capture ratios (91 and 87) suggest it tends to lag during strong market rallies, as expected by a value-oriented fund. At the same time, the downside capture ratios (104 and 88) paint a more nuanced picture, as the fund has declined more sharply over the past 3-year period, though this pattern has been in line with most peers. That said, while RPV has proved less volatile than the S&P 500, its efficiency as a defensive investment may very well be questioned, particularly over the past three years.

RPV’s average daily volume of $19.9 million is fair, but it can not be compared to large peers like VTV and IWD, which trade hundreds of millions of dollars per day. As a result, its bid-ask spread of 0.05% is relatively narrow, though it is wider than the very tight 0.01% spreads seen in those highly liquid funds. Its turnover of 42% is relatively high, especially when compared to peers such as VTV and MGV, which maintain much lower trading activity. This is reflected in its expense ratio of 0.35%, which is meaningfully higher than peers. The fund offers a relatively strong dividend yield of 2.41%, above both the peer group and the broader market. At the same time, its tracking error sits above peers, suggesting a less efficient index replication. Finally, RPV’s Sharpe ratios of 0.66 (3-year) and 0.44 (5-year) are at the lower end of the peer group range, reflecting a combination of modest performance without generating consistent lower volatility than peers.

Upside Drivers and Risk Considerations

RPV may have a better macro setup at this moment than it has had over the past few years. The combination of potential inflation pressures tied to elevated energy prices for a prolonged time and a more cautious tone around growth stocks has recently increased the appeal for value-oriented portfolios. That could be particularly applicable for a fund like RPV, given its deep-value design. If market leadership consistently broadens toward smaller capitalization companies and lower-multiple sectors, the fund may finally find a more favorable backdrop over the coming quarters.

The problem is that RPV’s portfolio has a structurally low position in technology and big techs that end up leaving the fund with a very modest growth profile. If big techs continue to lead overall market earnings growth, the fund should struggle to keep up with the broader market. On top of that, parts of the portfolio are cyclical by nature, such as basic materials, energy, and automakers. Thus, a sharper slowdown or worsening trade conditions could pressure returns somewhat. The other challenge is that RPV has not been as defensive, at least more recently. In the event of a sharper stock market sell-off, the fund may see steeper declines than broader equity indexes or more conservative peers.

Final Thoughts

Based on a strict value construction, RPV has its portfolio tilted toward some of the cheapest corners of the S&P 500. That gives it a deep value profile that is admittedly compelling at first sight, especially in a more risk-averse environment. However, investors must keep in mind that the fund’s heavy exposure to cyclical sectors may leave it vulnerable to economic downturns, while its weak growth profile has been a drag on the fund’s long-term performance and may continue to do so in generally bullish market conditions from here. In conclusion, I keep a neutral stance on this fund in the long run. However, assuming double-digit earnings growth for the S&P 500 in 2026, RPV can very well achieve a mid to high single-digit total return over the next twelve months, considering a limited space for multiple compression, given the fund’s very low valuation multiple.

data.title: Verizon: Why Shareholders Should Fear The Amazon Flywheel (Downgrade)
data.description:
It has been almost 5 months since I covered Verizon (VZ) and assigned a buy rating (check it here). And just in this period the market has already given a certain re-rating to the stock, which rose ~11%, representing a 14.5% total return after dividends.

This small upside is not enough to make me less optimistic about the stock, in my last article, I mentioned that the valuation was so appealing that even in a scenario where Verizon delivers a flat revenue forever growth, it would still be a case offering a double digits IRR.

But the most recent issue that made me more apprehensive was the news of Amazon (AMZN) acquiring Globalstar (GSAT). It may seem something not very important for now, at the moment Verizon has many more reasons to worry about traditional competition than about new players, but I believe that in the next 10 years this can change the game a lot and be a point of disruption.

Satellites As A Complement To Verizon Business Model

Before talking exactly about satellites and whether Amazon’s investment is a headwind or not, I will take a step back and talk about Verizon’s momentum.

It is a reasonable momentum for Verizon, I do not think it is possible to say it is super challenging or great. Looking at the operational side, Fixed wireless access broadband growing 25% draws attention, but wireless retail is growing 0.6% in connections, and this even with good metrics such as retail postpaid phone net adds of ~616 thousand (versus 504 in Q4 of 2024).

But looking at the 2026 guidance, we have a very modest number, nothing much. Around 2%~3% of revenue growth, with an adj. EPS of 4% or 5%.

That is, we are already talking about a Verizon that is not in that super favorable market momentum, there is not a huge tailwind. In fact, I believe it is much easier to argue that we are in a delicate moment. As much as the numbers seem solid enough for such a mature company, Verizon seems to already be at a stage and at a moment of the industry that has difficulties increasing ARPU and customers. For instance, if it increases price, churn increases (churn increased a few basis points in this quarter).

And this comes a lot from the competitive scenario, T-Mobile (TMUS) has several aggressive promotions, AT&T (T) also has large scale like Verizon to offer bundles and try to take market share, so often it is a fight where one tries to have a bit more differentiation, another tries to win on price, and in the end it is not a very healthy environment in terms of top line growth and margin expansion for the industry as a whole.

In the middle of this scenario, we have news like Amazon investing more than $11 billion in a satellite communication company and that strongly reinforces the thesis that eventually Amazon will be strong in this type of D2D (Direct-To-Device) connection. But starting with the bullish/neutral argument for Verizon, it is that satellite does not replace Verizon’s towers, in fact, they are something complementary in my opinion (but only in the short term).

If you stop to think, Verizon already has an extensive network of 4G-5G towers, and now it can make partnerships like it did with AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) to be able to provide signal where the tower does not reach, which is in very remote regions. So the core remains the same, but it can offer an additional service via these partnerships.

But the bullish side in my view is not even the complementarity side, but rather the economic viability of a satellite. Verizon’s entire expected CapEx for 2026 is $16 billion. Leasing a tower costs a few thousand dollars per month (of course there is the cost of building, which is a few tens of thousands of dollars). The useful life of the asset is enormous, although you have to continue with maintenance, management, and network expansion because a tower serves only one locality (let’s say, a coverage radius of a few miles).

Now, the cost of a satellite is around millions of USD, even for the smaller ones as you can see in the table below.

After producing, Amazon needs to take this satellite to orbit. A launch of the Atlas V Rocket costs ~$153 million. Of course, we are not talking about just 1 satellite at a time, but a few dozen units (like 29).

Besides that, we also have to think about the R&D issue. This type of operation is not something possible to be done overnight, Amazon and SpaceX (SPACE) spent a lot on R&D over years, decades, to make this viable, and will need to continue investing a lot to make this dream a reality.

Apple (AAPL) is a good example of using this D2D technology, using it for emergency SOS. And at the same time it reveals a difficulty. D2D is for different types of uses than broadband or 5G, we are talking about messages, emergency services, and not high-speed internet for you to watch YouTube.

I am saying all this to say that I do not think Verizon will be disrupted by Starlink or by Amazon in the next few years. I believe that the good old tower will still be efficient for some good years, and until someone manages to offer a product not only good enough, but also cheap enough, it may take some time.

However… There Are Risks For The Long-Run

But then comes the “but”. I think it will take time, but eventually it may indeed happen.

In my recent article about Amazon I talked about the flywheel and all the strength that Amazon’s ecosystem has (read it here). If other companies like Comcast (CMCSA) and AT&T already manage to make a bundle to try to offer more attractive products (mobile plan, cable TV, streaming and so on), think what Amazon could do. You are already an Amazon Prime subscriber, with a few more USD now you have D2D internet, anywhere in the world. It is a very large appeal. Even if we are not dealing with high-speed internet, it would already be enough for many people to subscribe.

But my concern is not even Amazon starting to offer this in 2 years. This should happen, but think what can happen in 10 years. Now Verizon’s towers seem fine. In 10 years the story changes completely.

As I mentioned, it is still a very costly operation, but at the same time, it also seems quite scalable. While a tower has a range of a few miles, a satellite can serve many more people at the same time and in many more different regions. Now add this to the constant R&D of Amazon/Globalstar/SpaceX. The natural path is that in ten years these satellites are more efficient to maintain, cheaper to launch (maybe they can take many at once instead of ~29), and still manage to offer quality internet directly to your smartphone.

And if Amazon manages to solve these problems, what happens to Verizon? I do not think that on the day Amazon manages to offer a good and cheap product Verizon will completely “disappear”, but the case becomes much more fragile. The “revenue flat” scenario becomes much harder to defend, because even if Verizon continues to exist, we are talking about companies that have the capacity to capture a lot of this market share and consequently significantly reduce several of Verizon’s revenues.

We should also remember that Verizon has a balance sheet that now looks healthy, but if the financials start to change, this “healthy” also changes. It is $158 billion in debt, with a net debt/EBITDA of 2.2x, low for a cash cow. But what if EBITDA starts to fall? What if the market starts pricing trends of constant declines in revenue (like a negative compounding of ~-10% from 2035 onward?) Then we have a complete change of profile.

In short, I do not think it is possible to be sure that in 10 years Verizon will be disrupted, maybe Amazon takes longer, and this type of delay in new technologies happens all the time, think about the self-driving vehicle that even though it is already a reality, for several years it was a promise that it would emerge in a short time. That is, maybe in 10 years Amazon is still doing R&D to try to make the business even more viable, maybe in 10 years the most that a “service” like this from Amazon does is allow sending messages in remote places, and in fact, it will take 20 or 30 years until Verizon is disrupted. In 20-30 years it is already enough for the company to generate enough shareholder yield to justify the whole thesis.

This is also true for VZ peers (when it comes to the long term). T-Mobile and AT&T, despite having different strategies, will likely have to reinvent themselves in the long run and also face the risk of disruption in this line of business. What changes is the medium term, since while VZ is partnering with AST and TMUS is partnering with Starlink/SpaceX, AT&T has a bit more predictability in terms of government contracts, so that changes the game a bit, but in any case, I believe that all of them will eventually have to reinvent themselves.

Bottom Line and Valuation

As I mentioned in the paragraph above and at the beginning of the article, I do not think Verizon is an overvalued company now. The ~9x fwd P/E still seems good enough for a cash cow, that is, as long as Verizon manages to maintain this revenue, preferably growing low single digits, and constantly distributes dividends and does buybacks, in the long run the IRR is positive.

A simple proxy to think about is: rounding to 10x earnings, we have 10% earnings yield. If net income grows even 1% every year, we already have an implied total return of more than 10% per year.

But still it is not a stock that seems so much more asymmetric to me. I do not see a scenario where the market gives a re-rating to 20x earnings or there is some growth that creates a considerable upside in the stock. But the negative side can happen, although I see a positive scenario as unlikely, in some years the market may start to price more clearly (as information comes out and things happen) the risk of disruption of Verizon, and in a scenario like this, it is a substantial downside.

Therefore, I am downgrading the rating to a hold, which means something more neutral, I still think VZ has shareholder return potential, but I do not think the scenario is as obvious anymore.

data.title: AeroVironment: Locust Laser Clears FAA Tests, Unlocking Growth Potential (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I’ve covered AeroVironment (AVAV) before, where I outlined the company’s background in detail and explained why I considered it an attractive buy. Since then, the stock has fallen by more than 25%, mainly due to weak Q3 numbers and a guidance revision; however, for investors following the hints, the news from the last few weeks has been great for the company and suggests that the LOCUST has completed safety trials with the US Department of War’s JIATF-401 task force and the FAA at White Sands. Positioning LOCUST as the first laser to be validated. I expect orders to commence shortly, propelling the company’s growth.

Background

AeroVironment is a defense technology company developing unmanned and directed energy systems for military use, and after the BlueHalo acquisition, it entered the space, cyber/electronic warfare, and laser communications segments. It is mainly known for small UAVs and counter-UAS solutions, with its laser tech, the LOCUST system, playing a pivotal role.

The SCDE division was impacted by the government shutdown; however, I expect it will be a huge contributor in the future, especially with its new LOCUST X3, which is capable of defeating Group 1–3 UAS threats at less than $5 per shot and can be integrated into fixed and mobile defense platforms. On the conference call, management commented:

Our LOCUST laser weapon system is performing well in the field in multiple theaters, and we're preparing to commercialize LOCUST to broaden our market base and while increasing production.

As LOCUST moves into higher volume production to meet the U.S. Army's needs, it will be a significant revenue driver for the company in the coming years.

Moreover, the system has completed safety trials with the US Department of War’s JIATF-401 task force and the FAA at White Sands. The recent agreement between the Pentagon and FAA to deploy counter-drone lasers near the Mexico border suggests LOCUST will be the first laser used, as it was the one tested, and AeroVironment announced that LOCUST has been involved in over 66 test events, safely engaging more than a thousand drones.

As mentioned by management, the company is moving into high-rate production to meet expected US Army requirements and to start to commercialize the laser for global customers. Moreover, management confirmed that LOCUST is being evaluated as part of the Golden Dome architecture, and it seems it is also being considered to protect airports and bases.

In summary, LOCUST X3 is now validated by both military tests and regulatory review (FAA), setting it up to be the first operational laser weapon against drones. Given these factors, LOCUST could open a new market and drive future revenue growth alongside current products.

Financial Position and Valuation

AeroVironment has a strong balance sheet with $587 million in cash and equivalents against $728 million in debt.

Q3 earnings were soft, and the company slightly lowered the guidance to a range of $1.85–$1.95 billion in revenue from the previous $1.95–$2.0B range. This was not well received by the market, as the stock dropped by 9%. The lower guidance was mainly due to the stop-work order related to the Space Scar program, commented on in the previous article, and the government shutdown. However, recent developments outlined in the background section make me optimistic that the company will continue growing in the future.

I still believe the company could reach $2.4 billion in revenue in FY 2027 (note that the company’s 2027 fiscal year has already started this April). However, given I expect positive news momentum from here, I’m upgrading the P/S ratio to 7x, which is more in line with peers:

That valuation implies a $16.8 billion market capitalization, or around $335 per share, representing almost a 70% upside. This leads me to upgrade the stock to a strong buy, and it has become one of my biggest positions.

Risks

The main risk is related to execution. AeroVironment is rapidly expanding production capacity for Switchblade drones, LOCUST lasers, and other platforms. Any delays or cost overruns could put pressure on margins and timelines. Possible export restrictions and international sales are another possible risk, as they could be affected by external factors.

Counter-UAS lasers also carry regulatory and safety risks. This is a new technology that is just beginning to be deployed. Although recent FAA tests have cleared LOCUST for US civil airspace use, any future incidents could delay broader deployments.

Finally, new competitors like nLIGHT (LASR) could jeopardize AeroVironment's first-mover advantage; however, for now nLIGHT is developing more powerful lasers for missile defense.

Conclusion

AeroVironment faces strong tailwinds with a proposed $1.5 trillion defense budget. Higher defense spending should flow directly to the company as it offers drone and counter-drone technology, which is crucial as demonstrated by recent wars.

I expect the LOCUST X3 laser to be an important growth driver for the company, justifying a fair value of at least $335 per share, representing almost a 70% upside potential.

data.title: Dorman Products Is Getting Close To An Upgrade
data.description:
To be honest with you, the last few months have been a difficult time for shareholders of Dorman Products, Inc. (DORM). Since I called the company a "Hold" back in January of this year, the stock has dropped 11.9%. That's far worse than the 0.7% drop that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. This is disappointing to say the least, especially as revenue for the company improved. The big problem seems to be profitability. Even though revenue is forecasted to rise through 2026, costs are going to increase further. That makes the company more expensive on a forward basis. But the good news here is that, on an absolute basis, the company is not exactly pricey. And even relative to other similar firms, it is between fairly valued and slightly undervalued. I am worried about the economy more broadly, and I do suspect that things will deteriorate in a way that will negatively impact the business more than what management is anticipating. And for that reason, I believe that maintaining it as a "Hold" candidate is appropriate. But it wouldn't take much more of a drop, or much in the way of operational improvement, for me to upgrade the stock.

Taking A Fresh Look At Dorman Products

For those not familiar with Dorman Products, the company operates as a provider of supplies such as replacement and upgrade parts for the motor vehicle aftermarket industry. A lot of what it does involves serving passenger cars. But it also touches on trucks of all sizes, specialty vehicles, and more. In fact, in 2025, the company marketed around 144,000 distinct parts. That actually represented an increase over the 138,000 that it marketed the previous year. And one thing that is certainly exciting about the business is that, while it does sell brand-name parts, 76% of the products that it sells fall under brands that it owns outright. This means greater operational control, lower risk, and likely larger profit margins in the long run.

The space that the company operates in is quite large. According to management, the light-duty, heavy-duty, and powersports markets, when focused solely on the aftermarket category, are worth a combined $165 billion. This is significant if you ask me. And it has allowed the company to establish for itself a great ecosystem by which it has been able to create meaningful shareholder value over the long run.

Currently, the newest data that investors have for the business covers through the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue amounted to $537.9 million. That represented an increase over the $533.8 million that the business reported a year earlier. This was great in and of itself. And it was driven by two of the three operating segments for the business. The laggard happened to be the Specialty Vehicle segment, with revenue flatlining at $53.5 billion. But honestly, that was even a bit of an improvement when you consider that, for the year as a whole, revenue dropped 3%. This weakness has been the result of a reduction in customer demand across its sales channels that more than offset higher prices. Management mentioned the impact of higher tariffs, which seemed to have been pushed in large part onto customers. It's also true that, while some parts of the specialty vehicle market have been coming back to life, like the RV industry, these spaces are highly susceptible to broader economic weakness. After all, these involve high-cost items that are discretionary in nature and that are often heavily financed.

By comparison, the other operating segments for the company have held up much better. Revenue for the Light Duty segment inched up from $427.4 million to $428.6 million year over year in the final quarter, with an even more impressive rise from $1.57 billion to $1.69 billion for each respective year as a whole. Management attributed this to increased customer demand in the first half of the year, strong demand for new products that the business continues to roll out, and higher prices that were absorbed by customers as management had to respond appropriately to tariffs. When it comes to new products that the company launched, management has provided some detail here. During 2025, the company introduced 5,560 distinct parts. Of those, 3,952 were line extensions, with another 1,608 classified as being new to the aftermarket. Examples of product launches have included exhaust manifolds, metal heater hose connectors, and aluminum oil filter housings. But there are plenty of other examples as well, as I'm sure you can imagine.

We also have the Heavy Duty segment, which saw revenue jump from $52.9 million in the final quarter of 2024 to $55.8 million at the same time in 2025, though for the year as a whole it barely budged from $231.5 million to $232.6 million. According to management, the sales increase that the company saw here was basically all because of tariff-induced price increases. Customer demand continued to be weak in the freight transportation and trucking aftermarkets. This is not surprising to me. As I have detailed in other articles, this industry is under significant pressure, entering into yet another year where there is the real prospect of a continuation of the recession that embroiled it not long after the COVID-19 pandemic came to an end.

My suspicion is that broader economic conditions will continue to deteriorate throughout this year. I have indicated as much in other articles, like here and here. Despite this, management seems optimistic. They are forecasting revenue growth of between 7% and 9%. Uncertainty regarding tariffs remains a problem. On top of this, management mentioned that shipment volume decline is continuing. However, toward the second half of this year, they do expect a recovery. But that won't be enough to avoid the company falling. If we take the midpoint of guidance when it comes to adjusted earnings per share for the year, we would get adjusted net profits for 2026 of $255.3 million compared to the $272.7 million that the business reported last year. Assuming that other profitability metrics scale down similarly, that would mean adjusted operating cash flow of $320.9 million and EBITDA of $385.7 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart below how shares of the company are priced right now. These are certainly not bad metrics. In fact, they would typically put the company in the undervalued category as I define it. Relative to other similar firms, the stock is probably a bit on the cheap side. That chart shows that it is cheaper than any of the five institutions that I compared it to on a price-to-earnings basis. But two of them are cheaper on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, while three of them are cheaper when it comes to the price-to-operating-cash-flow approach.

Takeaway

Long term, I would argue that the company will likely do quite well. Continued product introductions will certainly help to drive financial performance higher as time goes on. But this does not mean that investors should buy in just yet. With management even forecasting weakness in results this year and expressing in their latest earnings call worries over tariff uncertainty, caution is warranted even if it means missing out on short-term upside. Because of this, I believe that maintaining the company as a "Hold" candidate makes sense for now.

data.title: Marsh & McLennan: The Valuation May Finally Be Here
data.description:
In this article, I will be updating on Marsh & McLennan (MRSH), a company where I consider I have given a very accurate and valid sort of PT and fair value estimate. I can say this because in my last article on the company, I made a case for the company not being cheap enough just yet, and a $170/share price was the share price that you'd want to invest in.

The company broke through $170 a few days ago from the time of writing this piece, and traded for a day around $168/share, marking it a "Buy." As I am writing the article, the company has risen again, up to $181/share today as of 1Q26 earnings.

It's time to update my (correct) assessment for the company and see if it's finally time to upgrade this PT a bit. MRSH is a qualitative company with a very attractive set of business operations. But the company has unfortunately been chronically overvalued for over 15 years, as I see it. No company growing at 6%-9% per year should trade above 20x P/E - and this company has seen valuations of above 25x. This is especially true when its end market is so dependent on the market correlations we're seeing and when the entire market it serves (insurance) is under what I would consider quite problematic trends. The downside I forecasted based strictly on valuation was, in my mind, an inevitability.

And that's what I have been trying to communicate with you, dear readers. That MRSH is in fact a superb company - but that even superb companies like this one can't trade at any valuation and be called "fair" or accurately valued.

In this article, I will update my October thesis in light of the changing landscape in insurance brokering, and the company's new growth rate. Part of the company's premiumization journey has, in fact, been justified by a very solid double-digit growth rate in EPS for several years. That is now over. The current estimated growth rate for 2026 is only 6%, and I believe the coming growth rate estimates need to be discounted somewhat.

Let's see, therefore, what we have here.

Marsh & McLennan - Cautiously more optimistic as the company's normalization settles

It goes without saying that I believe the normalization the company has been through is completely justified. I have been saying for years that no insurance broker, regardless of A-rating, justified trading at this sort of multiple. The normalization proves, to me, that this was indeed something that happened over a few years but may not happen again.

The market has changed, and there are many indicators for this. MRSH is used to what in insurance vernacular is known as a "hard market." This is characterized not only by high premiums and overall a limited capacity but also by a market characterized by significant competitive pressure and far more complex business fundamentals. The operating environment for brokering insurance, despite operational growth, is pressured by sluggish rate increase trends, a significant rise in operational costs (for all businesses, not just insurance), and rapid digital adoption and transformational demands. This puts into question decades-old business models that will take many millions of dollars to change.

The biggest change is that after years of very favorable trends (which have been integral to MRSH's earnings growth), this growth is starting to ease off. Not in all segments - but things like prop insurance are continuing to soften. The current industry expectation is for markets to moderate in terms of growth.

It's important to recognize that all this decline represents for the company is a normalization of pre-growth cycle trends. The notion, which some analysts base their assumptions on, that things are going to go back to "supercycle" growth rate multiples, is inherently flawed.

Why?

Because neither the company, the markets, nor the forecasting analysts are expecting a return to those sorts of growth rates. In 201, the company grew AEPS by 21%. In 2026E, the current expectation is not even a third of that. In 2027E, it's not even half that - and I consider the 2027E-2028E expectations to be far too positive by half.

So what exactly is driving this decrease and this softening? What's going on in the market?

It's a combination of converging factors, really. You have things like input cost increases like wages and talent retention costs (comp and bonus), which, together with a softer premium environment, are creating a margin squeeze. To that, we have the introduction of AI systems into insurance, which are impacting the way that some insurance companies do underwriting and customer service. AI adoption is costly, and while MRSH can manage it, they can hardly manage it at those sorts of margins they previously had.

Then there are things I have been discussing when I've reviewed other insurance companies. MRSH is mainly a U.S.-based company. Its exposure to the heavily litigious U.S. insurance market - especially P&C - is facing increases in cost due to how court processes are done. The increases in NatCat and geopolitical volatility are all driving an environment where costs are higher. As a result, a company like this one has no choice but to raise rates and premiums - but there's only so much the consumer or customer can take. All of this speaks to this lower growth reality.

It doesn't help that MRSH has been part of inflating its own share price for many years, which meant that it took considerable market pressure to even drive the company down as such. In 2025, it's "decline year," the company spent a record amount on share repurchases - all of that money, given the decline in share price, is now "worth less than it was."

The strategy of buying back despite share price declines from an already overvalued level acts like a band-aid. The company essentially bought $9 for $10/share and has continued doing so during the year, wasting cash reserves and free cash flow. Not high marks from me. The results today, which saw the company's Non-GAAP beat estimates by 7 cents, are also not enough to declare the company a "victor" in any way. The growth rates - mostly around 3%-7% - continue to suggest that a lower growth environment is here.

As I said, I believe current analysts considering the stock attractive are making the mistake of looking at too short a timeframe. If you look at the long term, it becomes clear quickly that the market has been overvaluing this company too high for some time. SA Quant is, I believe, recognizing some of the issues with the company with clarity here, citing challenges in valuation, growth, and momentum - all things that I see as well and have based my neutral thesis on for several years.

As I said early on, the $170/share target was recently breached, and for a brief time, the company was a "Buy."

So can I justifiably increase my PT after what is, after all, quite a decent 1Q26 all things considered?

MRSH stock valuation - some increase, but not as much as it would take

I'd be willing to increase my PT a bit, but not as much as it would take for the company to become a "Buy" at $182/share, which is where the business is trading today.

The notion that the market is "overselling" this company even now, is one that I view as flawed. This notion does not consider two very fundamental changes in the market, which are unlikely to go away. First, the premium growth challenges in P&C, and second, the increased costs for the companies operating in these sectors. Anyone who seeks to understand this sector and the valuations for the companies in it needs to present a valuation estimate or consideration for this part of the risk/business change.

I can tell you with what I consider to be strong conviction that the historical premium growth rates are rates we'll see again in the near term. To counter social inflation, insurers and reinsurers need to strengthen their reserves and the way they provision for litigation losses. To counter AI investing, companies must set aside investment for this, and to counter NatCats, the companies need to provision differently, given the surging frequency of incidents with fires, floods, and hurricanes.

This market cycle is very different from previous insurance market cycles because, unlike historical market changes, this market softening is uneven. Unlike previous environments, not all the sub-sectors are dropping. This implies market bifurcation, which in turn implies that it's not just a cyclical up and down - it's a change in how P&C insurance and reinsurance consider risk for a sector.

As such, I consider discounting future growth rates for MMC to be a valid approach.

At the current valuation, the company's $182/share price dictates the company's annualized upside, even with an annually increased dividend at 2% (at the time of writing), is less than 15% annualized - but not a lot less. I consider my $170/share price to be slightly too low given the results and a 7%-8% growth rate average, together with a 20x P/E target (which, given the A- rating and the company's fundamentals, which I have detailed before, I consider to be fair).

I therefore increase my PT for the company to $177/share, at which point that 15% annualized, even at a 20x P/E and a 7%-8% growth rate, is possible.

Based on this assumption, I would therefore maintain "Hold," but given that this is an earnings day, the share price and valuation is fluid - so keep an eye on it, and if your hurdle is lower than 15% - say 13%, then it's possible to invest here at a valuation where I see that upside as possible.

However, I maintain that the market in general, as well as most analysts, are focusing too much on the 2014-2024 period - that period is gone, and I doubt, given the operational fundamentals that rule today, that we'll see it again anytime soon.

Risks & Considerations

As mentioned, MMC is a qualitative business, a leader in an interesting field. The risk to my thesis is that the company in fact recovers, and continues to grow, not at a 20x P/E, but at a 22-23x P/E, which would then bring about market-beating rates of return. This is entirely possible.

However, given the trends we've been seeing for the past 8-20 months, with a slow decline of the company's valuation, I would point more to the company's current risk profile to the downside - difficulties in the market, with softening policies and rates, as well as volatility in macro. Large market leaders like MMC are likely to do much better than others, but they will still be affected - and I maintain this view even after the positive 1Q26.

For that reason, I don't see the company's risk/reward profile as attractive at this time and would consider the company a "Hold" here with a slightly bumped PT of $177/share.

Thesis

With over 140+ years under its belt, this company is one of the largest out there in its respective businesses, and at a high valuation, this company becomes a no-nonsense "Buy" to me - at least at the right price (and it was only a week ago).

The company's trends after the most recent 1Q26, however, tell us that there is insufficient upside to the company and that my valuation and price target from my last article still hold. I therefore consider MMC no more than a "Hold" at an overvaluation. I do not see how you can get a positive return in excess of 13%-14% annually from this company. That is not enough for me, even if, at this time, it's very close.

As of this article in April 2026, I view the relevant PT for MMC to be a conservative $177/share, from which we are currently removed (if only slightly).

It's not a business you should buy here.

My 5 investment criteria are (italicized)

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $177/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.
